ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Viceroyalty of Peru was the principal Spanish colonial administration in South America, built on the conquered territories of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu) and dozens of other indigenous polities. The encomienda and mita systems constituted forced labor regimes that devastated indigenous populations. The mercury mining at Huancavelica and silver mining at Potosi killed hundreds of thousands of indigenous and enslaved African workers. Indigenous population collapse from disease, overwork, and violence reduced the pre-contact population of the Andean region by an estimated 80-90% within the first century of colonization. The viceroyalty's administrative boundaries were imposed without regard for pre-existing indigenous territorial organization.

name_variants.name: Viceroyalty of Peru
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Virreinato del Peru
name_variants.context: Official Spanish colonial name

name_variants.name: Vice-regnum Peruvianum
name_variants.context: Latin form used in official documents

territory_changes.region: Western South America from Panama to Tierra del Fuego
territory_changes.description: Established by the New Laws (Leyes Nuevas) of Charles V, consolidating Spanish territorial claims in South America following the conquest of the Inca Empire (1532-1533) and subsequent civil wars among the conquistadors. The viceroyalty initially encompassed virtually all Spanish claims in South America.

territory_changes.region: Northern South America (modern Colombia, Ecuador, Venezuela, Panama)
territory_changes.description: The Viceroyalty of New Granada was carved from the Viceroyalty of Peru, reducing its northern territories. This administrative reorganization under the Bourbon reforms reflected the difficulty of governing such vast territories from Lima.

The Battle of Ayacucho (December 9, 1824) ended Spanish authority in South America. The royalist forces under Viceroy Jose de la Serna were definitively defeated by the combined forces of Simon Bolivar and Antonio Jose de Sucre. The viceroyalty dissolved into the independent republics of Peru and Bolivia.

sources.citation: Andrien, Kenneth J. Crisis and Decline: The Viceroyalty of Peru in the Seventeenth Century. Albuquerque: University of New Mexico Press, 1985
Stern, Steve J. Peru's Indian Peoples and the Challenge of Spanish Conquest: Huamanga to 1640. Madison: University of Wisconsin Press, 1993
Fisher, John R. Bourbon Peru, 1750-1824. Liverpool: Liverpool University Press, 2003

name_original: Virreinato del Rio de la Plata
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata was created from territories previously under the Viceroyalty of Peru. Its establishment served Spanish mercantilist interests and the defense against Portuguese/Brazilian expansion. The territory encompassed lands of numerous indigenous peoples including the Guarani, Mapuche, Charrua, Diaguita, and Tehuelche, whose territorial sovereignty was not recognized. The Jesuit missions (reducciones) among the Guarani, while offering some protection from enslavement, also constituted a form of cultural transformation. The Spanish silver trade from Potosi, which economically justified the viceroyalty, was built on coerced indigenous labor under the mita system.

name_variants.name: Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Virreinato del Rio de la Plata
name_variants.context: Official Spanish name

name_variants.name: Vice-royaute du Rio de la Plata
name_variants.context: French designation used in contemporary diplomatic correspondence

territory_changes.region: Southern South America (modern Argentina, Uruguay, Paraguay, Bolivia)
territory_changes.description: Created by Charles III as part of the Bourbon administrative reforms, separating the territories of Upper Peru (Charcas), Paraguay, Buenos Aires, and the Banda Oriental from the Viceroyalty of Peru. The reorganization aimed to improve defense against Portuguese-Brazilian encroachment and better control the silver trade from Potosi.

British invasions of Buenos Aires (1806) and Montevideo (1807) were repelled by local militias without Spanish reinforcement, demonstrating criollo military capacity and undermining confidence in Spanish protection. This catalyzed independence sentiment.

The May Revolution (Revolucion de Mayo) deposed the viceroy in Buenos Aires, establishing a governing junta. While nominally maintaining allegiance to Ferdinand VII, this effectively ended viceregal authority. The viceroyalty fragmented as Paraguay, the Banda Oriental, and Upper Peru pursued separate paths.

sources.citation: Lynch, John. Spanish Colonial Administration, 1782-1810: The Intendant System in the Viceroyalty of the Rio de la Plata. London: Athlone Press, 1958
Rock, David. Argentina, 1516-1987: From Spanish Colonization to Alfonsin. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1987

boundary_aourednik_name: Viceroyalty of the Río de la Plata

capital_name: Santiago de los Caballeros de Guatemala
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Captaincy General of Guatemala administered the territories of numerous Maya peoples (K'iche', Kaqchikel, Tz'utujil, Mam, Q'eqchi', and many others), as well as Lenca, Pipil, Chorotega, and other indigenous nations. The Spanish conquest of Guatemala under Pedro de Alvarado (1524) was exceptionally brutal. Forced labor under the repartimiento system, disease epidemics, and cultural suppression through the extirpation of indigenous religion devastated these communities. The capital was destroyed by earthquakes multiple times, reflecting the hazardous geography. Maya resistance continued throughout the colonial period, including the long-unconquered Itza Maya of Tayasal (not conquered until 1697).

name_variants.name: Captaincy General of Guatemala
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Capitania General de Guatemala
name_variants.context: Official Spanish name

name_variants.name: Reino de Guatemala
name_variants.context: Informal designation as the Kingdom of Guatemala

territory_changes.region: Central America from Chiapas to Costa Rica
territory_changes.description: Formally established as an audiencia with expanded military authority. The captaincy general administered the provinces of Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, Costa Rica, and Chiapas (now part of Mexico), technically subordinate to the Viceroyalty of New Spain but with substantial autonomy.

The Santa Marta earthquakes destroyed the capital Santiago de los Caballeros (now Antigua Guatemala). The capital was relocated to the Valley of the Hermitage, founding Nueva Guatemala de la Asuncion (modern Guatemala City) in 1776.

Independence declared on September 15, 1821, largely by criollo elites in Guatemala City. The declaration was influenced by the success of Mexican independence. The territory briefly annexed to the First Mexican Empire before forming the Federal Republic of Central America in 1823.

sources.citation: Wortman, Miles. Government and Society in Central America, 1680-1840. New York: Columbia University Press, 1982
Lovell, W. George. Conquest and Survival in Colonial Guatemala: A Historical Geography of the Cuchumatan Highlands, 1500-1821. Montreal: McGill-Queen's University Press, 2005

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Captaincy General of Cuba was Spain's longest-held American colony. The indigenous Taino and Ciboney populations were effectively annihilated within decades of Spanish arrival through disease, enslavement, and violence. The colonial economy was subsequently built on the massive importation of enslaved Africans for sugar and tobacco production. Cuba became the world's largest sugar producer in the 19th century through slave labor, with an estimated 800,000 Africans forcibly brought to the island. Slavery was not abolished in Cuba until 1886. The Ten Years' War (1868-1878) and the War of Independence (1895-1898) resulted in massive civilian casualties and Spain's use of reconcentracion camps, a precursor to 20th-century concentration camps.

name_variants.name: Captaincy General of Cuba
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Capitania General de Cuba
name_variants.context: Official Spanish name

name_variants.name: Siempre Fidelisima Isla de Cuba
name_variants.context: 'Ever Most Faithful Island of Cuba', honorific title reflecting Cuba's loyalty during mainland independence wars

territory_changes.description: Havana designated as the official capital, consolidating Cuba's role as the strategic hub of Spain's American empire. The city served as the assembly point for the treasure fleet (flota) system, making it one of the most fortified cities in the Americas.

British forces captured Havana during the Seven Years' War, occupying the city for 11 months. The occupation opened Cuba to free trade and introduced thousands of enslaved Africans. Spain recovered Cuba in exchange for Florida under the Treaty of Paris (1763).

The Spanish-American War ended Spanish sovereignty. Despite decades of Cuban independence struggle (1868-1898), the Treaty of Paris transferred Cuba to U.S. military occupation rather than recognizing full Cuban sovereignty. The Platt Amendment (1901) further limited Cuban independence until its abrogation in 1934.

sources.citation: Thomas, Hugh. Cuba, or The Pursuit of Freedom. New York: Harper & Row, 1971
Ferrer, Ada. Freedom's Mirror: Cuba and Haiti in the Age of Revolution. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2014

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Province of Carolina was established on lands inhabited by the Catawba, Cherokee, Tuscarora, Yamasee, Waccamaw, and other indigenous nations. English colonization involved treaties that were systematically violated, armed conquest, and the deliberate use of inter-tribal conflicts. The Carolina colony was heavily involved in the indigenous slave trade; an estimated 30,000-50,000 indigenous people were enslaved and exported to the Caribbean and other colonies between 1670 and 1715. The colony also became a major center of African chattel slavery, particularly for rice cultivation in the Lowcountry. The Yamasee War (1715-1717) was a direct response to English abuses including enslavement, trade fraud, and land encroachment. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1700, strategy=capital_near_centroid, precision=3). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Province of Carolina
name_variants.context: Official English colonial designation

name_variants.name: Carolina Colony
name_variants.context: Common informal designation

name_variants.name: Carolana
name_variants.context: Earlier name from the 1629 Heath patent, named for Charles I

territory_changes.region: Southeastern North America from Virginia to Spanish Florida
territory_changes.description: Charles II granted a charter to eight Lords Proprietors for the territory between 31 and 36 degrees north latitude. The grant ignored existing indigenous sovereignty and overlapped with Spanish claims. Settlement began with the founding of Charles Town in 1670.

The Yamasee War nearly destroyed the Carolina colony. A coalition of Yamasee, Creek, Catawba, and other nations attacked English settlements in response to decades of enslavement, trade abuses, and land theft. The war killed approximately 7% of South Carolina's colonial population and displaced frontier settlements.

The colony was formally divided into the royal colonies of North Carolina and South Carolina after the Lords Proprietors sold their charter back to the Crown. The division reflected long-standing administrative and economic differences between the northern Albemarle settlements and the southern Lowcountry centered on Charles Town.

sources.citation: Gallay, Alan. The Indian Slave Trade: The Rise of the English Empire in the American South, 1670-1717. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2002
Edgar, Walter. South Carolina: A History. Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1998

ethical_notes: ETHICS: New France encompassed the territories of hundreds of indigenous nations including the Wendat (Huron), Algonquin, Innu (Montagnais), Mi'kmaq, Ojibwe, Odawa, Potawatomi, Illinois, and many others. While French colonial relations with indigenous peoples involved more diplomatic alliance and trade partnership than the English model of settlement displacement, the French also introduced devastating epidemic diseases, disrupted indigenous political systems through the fur trade, and employed Jesuit missionaries whose explicit goal was the destruction of indigenous spiritual practices. The French-allied destruction of the Wendat Confederacy by the Haudenosaunee (1649) was partly a consequence of French-driven competition for fur trade access. The deportation of the Acadians (1755-1764) by the British after conquest constitutes an act of ethnic cleansing. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1650, strategy=exact_name, precision=3). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: New France
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Nouvelle-France
name_variants.context: Official French colonial name

name_variants.name: Nova Francia
name_variants.context: Latin form used in maps and official documents

territory_changes.description: Jacques Cartier claimed the St. Lawrence region for France. Permanent settlement began with Samuel de Champlain's founding of Quebec in 1608. French territorial claims eventually extended from the Atlantic coast through the Great Lakes to the Gulf of Mexico, though actual settlement was sparse.

territory_changes.region: Mississippi River valley to the Gulf of Mexico
territory_changes.description: Rene-Robert Cavelier de La Salle claimed the entire Mississippi River basin for France, naming it Louisiana. This vastly expanded New France's nominal territory, though French presence consisted mainly of trading posts and missions among allied indigenous nations.

territory_changes.region: All French territories in mainland North America
territory_changes.description: The Treaty of Paris (1763) ending the Seven Years' War ceded all French territories east of the Mississippi to Britain and Louisiana west of the Mississippi to Spain. Approximately 70,000 French colonists in Canada came under British rule. The deportation of Acadians had begun in 1755.

sources.citation: Eccles, W. J. The French in North America, 1500-1783. East Lansing: Michigan State University Press, 1998
White, Richard. The Middle Ground: Indians, Empires, and Republics in the Great Lakes Region, 1650-1815. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1991
Havard, Gilles, and Cecile Vidal. Histoire de l'Amerique francaise. Paris: Flammarion, 2003

ethical_notes:
ETHICS: Dutch Brazil was established through military conquest of Portuguese colonial territory in northeastern Brazil. The colony's economy was entirely dependent on enslaved African labor in sugar production. Under Johan Maurits van Nassau-Siegen (1637-1644), the colony experienced relative religious tolerance (including for Jews and Catholics) and scientific/artistic patronage, but this coexisted with the continuation and expansion of the sugar plantation slave economy. The Dutch West India Company was simultaneously one of the largest slave-trading enterprises in the Atlantic world. After the Dutch were expelled, Portuguese authorities persecuted the Sephardic Jewish community that had flourished under Dutch rule, many of whom fled to Dutch colonies in the Caribbean and to New Amsterdam. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1650, strategy=exact_name, precision=3). CC BY 4.0.

[v6.30-displaced-rollback] Boundary reverted to capital-based approximation: aourednik fuzzy match placed polygon centroid=2032km / edge=1346km from capital (exceeded thresholds). Regenerated as 300.0km radius circle.

name_variants.name: Dutch Brazil
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Nieuw-Holland
name_variants.context: Official Dutch colonial name: 'New Holland'

name_variants.name: Brasil Holandes
name_variants.context: Portuguese/Brazilian designation

territory_changes.description: The Dutch West India Company captured Olinda and Recife, establishing a colonial foothold. Dutch control eventually extended from the Sao Francisco River to Maranhao, encompassing the most productive sugar-producing region of the Americas.

Under Governor-General Johan Maurits van Nassau-Siegen, Dutch Brazil reached its maximum extent, controlling seven of the fourteen Portuguese captaincies. Maurits founded Mauritsstad (modern Recife) as the colonial capital and invited scientists, artists, and cartographers to document the colony.

After years of Portuguese-Brazilian insurgency (the Insurreicao Pernambucana, 1645-1654), Dutch forces surrendered Recife. The Battles of Guararapes (1648, 1649) were decisive victories for the Portuguese-Brazilian forces, which included indigenous and Afro-Brazilian fighters. The Dutch formally ceded claims to Brazil in 1661.

sources.citation: Boxer, Charles R. The Dutch in Brazil, 1624-1654. Oxford: Clarendon Press, 1957
Mello, Evaldo Cabral de. Olinda Restaurada: Guerra e Acucar no Nordeste, 1630-1654. Sao Paulo: Editora 34, 2007

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Peru-Bolivia Confederation represented an attempt by Andres de Santa Cruz to reunite territories that had been part of the Inca Empire and the Viceroyalty of Peru. The confederation was seen as a threat to regional power balances by Chile and Argentina, who waged the War of the Confederation (1836-1839) to destroy it. The indigenous Quechua and Aymara populations, who constituted the majority in both Peru and Bolivia, were largely excluded from the political process that created and destroyed the confederation. Santa Cruz himself was of mixed Aymara-Spanish descent, making him one of the few non-white heads of state in 19th-century South America.

name_variants.name: Peru-Bolivian Confederation
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Confederacion Peru-Boliviana
name_variants.context: Official Spanish name

name_variants.name: Confederation des Andes
name_variants.context: French diplomatic designation

territory_changes.description: Andres de Santa Cruz, President of Bolivia, invaded Peru and established the confederation composed of three states: the State of North Peru, the State of South Peru, and Bolivia. Santa Cruz served as Supreme Protector. The confederation was proclaimed at Tacna on October 28, 1836.

Chilean and Peruvian restorationist forces defeated Santa Cruz at the Battle of Yungay (January 20, 1839), dissolving the confederation. Chile opposed the confederation as a regional rival, while Argentine Confederation under Rosas opposed it from the south. Santa Cruz was exiled to Ecuador.

sources.citation: Parkerson, Phillip T. Andres de Santa Cruz y la Confederacion Peru-Boliviana, 1835-1839. La Paz: Libreria Editorial Juventud, 1984
Sobrevilla Perea, Natalia. The Caudillo of the Andes: Andres de Santa Cruz. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 2011

name_original: Provincias Unidas del Rio de la Plata
ethical_notes: ETHICS: The United Provinces emerged from the independence movement against Spanish colonial rule but inherited the colonial territory's disregard for indigenous sovereignty. The independence-era campaigns in the Chaco, Patagonia, and the Pampas represented the continuation of territorial expansion at the expense of indigenous peoples. Internal conflicts between Buenos Aires centralists (unitarios) and provincial federalists reflected deep economic and political inequalities. The territories of Paraguay and the Banda Oriental (Uruguay) rejected Buenos Aires's authority, and their independence represents the failure of the unitary project. Indigenous peoples such as the Ranquel, Mapuche, and Charrua were not represented in the independence process.

name_variants.name: United Provinces of the Rio de la Plata
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: United Provinces of South America
name_variants.context: Name used in the 1816 Declaration of Independence at Tucuman

name_variants.name: Provincias Unidas en Sud America
name_variants.context: Official name used in the Congress of Tucuman declaration

territory_changes.description: The May Revolution established the Primera Junta, deposing the last viceroy Baltasar Hidalgo de Cisneros. The Congress of Tucuman formally declared independence on July 9, 1816, under the name 'United Provinces in South America.'

The Battle of Cepeda (February 1, 1820) defeated the central government's forces, dissolving the directorial government. The provincial caudillos asserted sovereignty, beginning a period of provincial autonomy. Paraguay had already effectively separated in 1811, and the Banda Oriental was under Portuguese-Brazilian occupation.

The Federal Pact (Pacto Federal) signed by Buenos Aires, Entre Rios, and Santa Fe established a loose confederation, effectively replacing the United Provinces framework. This marked the beginning of the Argentine Confederation period under Juan Manuel de Rosas's dominance.

sources.citation: Halperin Donghi, Tulio. Politics, Economics, and Society in Argentina in the Revolutionary Period. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1975
Shumway, Nicolas. The Invention of Argentina. Berkeley: University of California Press, 1991

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Quito is a contested historical concept. The 18th-century Jesuit historian Juan de Velasco described it as a pre-Inca polity that was conquered by the Inca through marriage alliance and then warfare. Modern archaeologists and historians (Frank Salomon, Tamara Bray) have questioned whether a unified 'Kingdom of Quito' existed, arguing that the pre-Inca highlands of Ecuador were organized into smaller chiefdoms (seniorios) such as the Cara, Panzaleo, and Puruhae, rather than a centralized state. The concept of a unified Kingdom of Quito has been important to Ecuadorian national identity, and rejecting it outright risks dismissing indigenous political complexity, while accepting it uncritically perpetuates potentially fabricated history. Both perspectives must be represented.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Quito
name_variants.context: Standard English designation from Juan de Velasco's account

name_variants.name: Kitu
name_variants.context: Quechua rendering of the indigenous toponym

name_variants.name: Shyri Kingdom
name_variants.context: Alternative designation referencing the alleged ruling dynasty, the Shyri (Scyri)

territory_changes.description: Traditional date for the Cara invasion of the Quito basin and establishment of the Shyri dynasty, as described by Juan de Velasco. The historicity of this event is disputed; archaeologists find no clear evidence of a single conquering event. The region was likely organized into multiple competing chiefdoms (seniorios).

The Inca under Tupac Inca Yupanqui conquered the highland chiefdoms of Ecuador after prolonged military campaigns. The integration of the Quito region into Tawantinsuyu transformed it into the northern administrative center of the empire. The Canari people, who resisted Inca conquest, were subjected to forced resettlement (mitmaq).

sources.citation: Velasco, Juan de. Historia del Reino de Quito en la America Meridional (1789). Quito: Imprenta del Gobierno, 1841-1844
Salomon, Frank. Native Lords of Quito in the Age of the Incas: The Political Economy of North Andean Chiefdoms. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1986
Bray, Tamara L. 'The Archaeology of the Ecuadorian Formative.' Journal of World Prehistory 17, no. 3 (2003): 219-263

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Muzo people of the Magdalena River valley in central Colombia are poorly documented in pre-colonial sources, and much of what is recorded comes from Spanish colonial accounts written by their conquerors, who characterized them as especially fierce and 'uncivilized' to justify violent subjugation. The Muzo controlled the world's most significant emerald deposits (Boyaca region), which made their territory a target for Spanish conquest. The Muzo resisted Spanish colonization longer than most Colombian indigenous groups, fighting a series of wars from the 1530s to the 1550s. Their ultimate defeat led to enslavement in emerald mines and near-total population destruction. The Muzo language is now extinct.

name_variants.name: Muzo Territory
name_variants.context: English designation for the Muzo political territory

name_variants.name: Muzos
name_variants.context: Spanish colonial designation for the people and their territory

name_variants.name: Land of the Emeralds
name_variants.context: Informal reference to the emerald-rich Muzo territory in Boyaca

territory_changes.description: Approximate period of Muzo settlement consolidation in the Magdalena River valley west of the Muisca territories. The Muzo controlled a territory rich in emeralds and maintained a distinct cultural and linguistic identity from their Muisca and Panche neighbors. Dating is approximate based on archaeological evidence.

After decades of failed Spanish attempts at conquest (beginning in 1539 under Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada), the Muzo were finally subjugated by Captain Luis Lanchero. The Muzo had inflicted significant casualties on Spanish forces over twenty years of resistance. Conquest led to forced labor in emerald mines and catastrophic population decline.

sources.citation: Langebaek, Carl Henrik. Mercados, Poblamiento e Integracion Etnica entre los Muiscas, Siglo XVI. Bogota: Banco de la Republica, 1987
Francis, J. Michael. Invading Colombia: Spanish Accounts of the Gonzalo Jimenez de Quesada Expedition of Conquest. University Park: Penn State University Press, 2007

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Tarascan State (Purepecha Empire) was the second-largest polity in Mesoamerica at the time of European contact and the only state that successfully resisted Aztec (Mexica) expansion. The Purepecha people have their own distinct language, unrelated to any other known language family, and a distinct cultural tradition. Spanish colonial and Mexica/Aztec sources both carry significant biases: Aztec accounts vilify the Tarascan as rivals, while Spanish accounts were written to justify conquest. The Spanish conquest under Nuno de Guzman (1530) was exceptionally brutal, including the torture and execution of the last cazonci (king) Tangaxoan II. The modern Purepecha community continues to maintain its language and cultural identity. Using the term 'Tarascan' is debated, as it may derive from a Spanish misunderstanding; 'Purepecha' is preferred by the community. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1400, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Tarascan State
name_variants.context: Traditional English academic designation, though potentially pejorative

name_variants.name: Purepecha Empire
name_variants.context: Preferred designation using the people's own name for themselves

name_variants.name: Irecha Irechekwa
name_variants.context: Purepecha term for the kingdom/state, meaning 'the lord's domain'

name_variants.name: Michoacan
name_variants.context: Nahuatl name meaning 'place of the fishermen', used by the Aztecs

territory_changes.description: The Purepecha, under the Uacusecha dynasty, consolidated control over the Lake Patzcuaro basin. According to the Relacion de Michoacan (c. 1540), the founder Tariacuri unified the Purepecha through a combination of conquest and alliance. Tzintzuntzan became the capital and ceremonial center.

The Purepecha decisively defeated the Aztec Triple Alliance at the Battle of Taximaroa, preventing Aztec expansion into western Mexico. This was one of the few major military defeats suffered by the Aztec Empire and established the Tarascan-Aztec frontier that persisted until Spanish arrival.

Spanish forces under Nuno Beltran de Guzman invaded and conquered the Purepecha state. The last cazonci, Tangaxoan II, had initially submitted peacefully to Hernan Cortes in 1522, but Guzman had him tortured and burned alive in 1530 to extract gold. The conquest was condemned even by Spanish contemporaries for its brutality.

sources.citation: Pollard, Helen Perlstein. Tariacuri's Legacy: The Prehispanic Tarascan State. Norman: University of Oklahoma Press, 1993
Relacion de Michoacan (c. 1540). Edited by Leoncio Cabrero. Madrid: Historia 16, 1989
Afanador-Pujol, Angelica. The Relacion de Michoacan (1539-1541) and the Politics of Representation in Colonial Mexico. Austin: University of Texas Press, 2015

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Kingdom of Cusco represents the formative period of Inca civilization before its imperial expansion under Pachacuti. Much of the early history is derived from Inca oral traditions recorded by Spanish chroniclers (Garcilaso de la Vega, Cieza de Leon, Betanzos), which blend mythological and historical elements. The traditional list of eight pre-imperial sinkchi (war leaders) and sapa incas before Pachacuti is difficult to verify archaeologically. Distinguishing historical from mythological rulers is a matter of ongoing scholarly debate. The early Cusco polity engaged in localized warfare and alliance-building with neighboring groups in the Cusco Valley. Boundary da aourednik/historical-basemaps (1300, strategy=capital_in_polygon, precision=1). CC BY 4.0.

name_variants.name: Kingdom of Cusco
name_variants.context: Standard English designation for the pre-imperial Inca polity

name_variants.name: Qusqu
name_variants.context: Quechua name for the city and polity, meaning possibly 'navel' or 'center'

name_variants.name: Curacazgo del Cusco
name_variants.context: Spanish academic term emphasizing its chiefdom-level organization before imperial expansion

territory_changes.description: Approximate period of Inca settlement in the Cusco Valley. According to Inca oral tradition, the legendary founder Manco Capac led the Inca people to Cusco from Lake Titicaca or from the cave of Tampu T'uqu. Archaeological evidence confirms increasing social complexity and settlement consolidation in the Cusco Valley during the 13th century.

Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui defeated the Chanka confederation, which had besieged Cusco, in the Battle of Yahuarpampa. This pivotal victory transformed Cusco from a regional kingdom into the center of the rapidly expanding Tawantinsuyu (Inca Empire). Pachacuti subsequently rebuilt Cusco as an imperial capital and launched campaigns of conquest throughout the Andes.

sources.citation: Bauer, Brian S. The Development of the Inca State. Austin: University of Texas Press, 1992
Covey, R. Alan. How the Incas Built Their Heartland: State Formation and the Innovation of Imperial Strategies in the Sacred Valley, Peru. Ann Arbor: University of Michigan Press, 2006
Garcilaso de la Vega, Inca. Comentarios Reales de los Incas (1609). Edited by Carlos Aranibar. Lima: Fondo de Cultura Economica, 1991

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Confederation of the Equator was a short-lived republican revolt in northeastern Brazil against the centralizing tendencies of Emperor Pedro I. The movement drew on earlier republican traditions (the 1817 Pernambucan Revolt) and was driven by provincial elites, liberal intellectuals, and urban populations frustrated by imperial authoritarianism. The revolt was brutally suppressed; leader Frei Caneca was executed by firing squad after the hangman refused to execute him. While the revolt expressed legitimate demands for federalism and constitutional governance, its leadership remained within the slaveholding planter class and did not challenge the institution of slavery that sustained the regional economy.

name_variants.name: Confederation of the Equator
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Confederacao do Equador
name_variants.context: Portuguese name, referencing the proximity to the equator

name_variants.name: Republica do Equador
name_variants.context: Alternative designation emphasizing its republican aspirations

territory_changes.region: Northeastern Brazil (Pernambuco, Ceara, Rio Grande do Norte, Paraiba)
territory_changes.description: On July 2, 1824, Manuel de Carvalho Pais de Andrade proclaimed the Confederation of the Equator in Recife, rejecting the centralist Constitution imposed by Pedro I after he dissolved the Constituent Assembly. The confederation sought to unite the northeastern provinces into an independent republic.

Imperial forces, including a naval blockade and mercenary forces under Lord Cochrane, suppressed the rebellion by September 1824. Frei Joaquim do Amor Divino Caneca, the intellectual leader, was captured and executed on January 13, 1825. Other leaders were imprisoned or exiled.

sources.citation: Mello, Evaldo Cabral de. A Outra Independencia: O Federalismo Pernambucano de 1817 a 1824. Sao Paulo: Editora 34, 2004
Barman, Roderick J. Brazil: The Forging of a Nation, 1798-1852. Stanford: Stanford University Press, 1988

ethical_notes: ETHICS: Gran Colombia was Simon Bolivar's most ambitious political project, uniting the former Viceroyalty of New Granada into a single republic. The state was founded on Enlightenment ideals of republicanism but maintained significant social hierarchies. Slavery was not immediately abolished; Bolivar's free-womb law (1821) freed children born to enslaved mothers but maintained adult slavery. Indigenous communities were granted nominal citizenship but lost communal land protections. The republic's dissolution into Venezuela, Ecuador, and New Granada reflected deep regional, economic, and political divisions that Bolivar's centralizing vision could not overcome. Bolivar's dictatorship in his final years remains a subject of intense historical debate.

name_variants.name: Gran Colombia
name_variants.context: Standard English designation to distinguish from modern Colombia

name_variants.name: Republic of Colombia
name_variants.context: Official English translation of the state's name

name_variants.name: Republica de Colombia
name_variants.context: Official name as established at the Congress of Angostura (1819) and Constitution of Cucuta (1821)

territory_changes.description: Following Bolivar's victory at Boyaca (August 7, 1819), the Congress of Angostura proclaimed the union of Venezuela and New Granada as the Republic of Colombia on December 17, 1819. Ecuador was incorporated after the Battle of Pichincha (May 24, 1822).

Antonio Jose de Sucre's victory at the Battle of Pichincha liberated Quito and incorporated the former Audiencia of Quito into Gran Colombia. Panama had already declared independence from Spain and joined Gran Colombia in 1821.

Venezuela seceded under Jose Antonio Paez in 1829-1830, and Ecuador under Juan Jose Flores in May 1830. Bolivar resigned the presidency on March 1, 1830, and died on December 17, 1830. The remaining territory reconstituted itself as the Republic of New Granada in 1831.

sources.citation: Bushnell, David. The Santander Regime in Gran Colombia. Newark: University of Delaware Press, 1954
Lynch, John. Simon Bolivar: A Life. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2006

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Federal Republic of Central America was an attempt to maintain the unity of the former Captaincy General of Guatemala as an independent federal state. The federation was dominated by criollo and ladino (mestizo) elites; indigenous Maya, Lenca, Garifuna, and other peoples constituted the demographic majority but were excluded from political participation. The liberal-conservative civil wars that tore the federation apart were fought largely by indigenous and mestizo soldiers conscripted by both sides. Rafael Carrera's revolt (1837), which eventually destroyed the federation, drew significant support from indigenous communities opposed to liberal reforms that threatened communal lands and traditional structures. The federation's failure shaped the political fragmentation of Central America that persists today.

name_variants.name: Federal Republic of Central America
name_variants.context: Standard English designation

name_variants.name: Republica Federal de Centro America
name_variants.context: Official Spanish name

name_variants.name: Provincias Unidas del Centro de America
name_variants.context: Earlier designation as United Provinces of Central America (1823-1824)

territory_changes.region: Central America from Guatemala to Costa Rica
territory_changes.description: Following the collapse of the First Mexican Empire, the former Central American provinces declared absolute independence on July 1, 1823. The National Constituent Assembly established the federation comprising Guatemala, El Salvador, Honduras, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica. The liberal constitution of 1824 modeled on the U.S. system proved ill-suited to Central American conditions.

Nicaragua seceded in November 1838, followed by Honduras and Costa Rica. The federal congress effectively ceased to function. Francisco Morazan, the last federal president, attempted military reunification from El Salvador but failed.

Morazan's final defeat and exile (1840), followed by his execution in Costa Rica (1842 after a failed comeback), ended any realistic prospect of federal reunification. El Salvador, the last state to maintain federation, declared itself a sovereign republic. Numerous subsequent reunification attempts all failed.

sources.citation: Woodward, Ralph Lee, Jr. Central America: A Nation Divided. Oxford: Oxford University Press, 1999
Dym, Jordana. From Sovereign Villages to National States: City, State, and Federation in Central America, 1759-1839. Albuquerque: University of New Mexico Press, 2006

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Comanche Empire (Comancheria) was one of the most powerful indigenous polities in North American history, dominating the southern Great Plains for over a century. The Comanche disrupted Spanish colonial expansion, defeated the Apache from the southern plains, and forced Mexican, Texan, and American states into diplomatic accommodation. The term 'empire' is used by historian Pekka Hamalainen to challenge the narrative that only European-derived states could exercise imperial power. The Comanche economy included raiding for horses and captives from Mexican and other indigenous settlements; captive-taking and slavery were integral parts of Comanche society, though captives could also be adopted. The Comanche were ultimately devastated by the deliberate U.S. military destruction of the bison herds, epidemic disease (especially cholera and smallpox), and relentless military campaigns that constitute genocide. The Red River War (1874-1875) forced the last free Comanche bands onto reservations.

name_variants.name: Comanche Empire
name_variants.context: Term coined by Pekka Hamalainen emphasizing the polity's imperial nature

name_variants.name: Comancheria
name_variants.context: Spanish/Mexican designation for the Comanche-controlled territory

name_variants.name: Numunuu
name_variants.context: Comanche autonym meaning 'the people'

territory_changes.region: Southern Great Plains from Kansas to Texas
territory_changes.description: The Comanche, originally a Shoshone-speaking group from the Rocky Mountains, migrated onto the southern plains after acquiring horses. By 1700 they began displacing the Apache and establishing dominance over a vast territory. The horse-centered economy transformed them into the dominant military and economic power of the southern plains.

The Comanche Peace of 1786 with Spanish New Mexico, negotiated by Governor Juan Bautista de Anza and Comanche chief Ecueracapa, established a diplomatic framework that held for decades. This represented a Spanish acknowledgment of Comanche sovereignty and military parity.

The Red River War (1874-1875) ended Comanche independence. Quanah Parker and the last free Kwahadi band surrendered at Fort Sill in June 1875. The systematic destruction of the southern bison herd by commercial hunters, tacitly encouraged by the U.S. military, destroyed the economic basis of Comanche life. Surviving Comanche were confined to a reservation in Oklahoma.

sources.citation: Hamalainen, Pekka. The Comanche Empire. New Haven: Yale University Press, 2008
Kavanagh, Thomas W. Comanche Political History: An Ethnohistorical Perspective, 1706-1875. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1996

ethical_notes: ETHICS: The Muscogee (Creek) Confederacy was one of the largest and most politically sophisticated indigenous polities in southeastern North America. The confederacy was a voluntary union of diverse towns (talwa) that maintained significant internal autonomy while cooperating in diplomacy and war. English, French, and Spanish colonizers all attempted to manipulate Creek diplomacy for their own purposes, and the Muscogee skillfully played European powers against each other. The Creek War (1813-1814), often framed in U.S. history as a minor conflict, was a devastating civil war within the confederacy catalyzed by pressures of American expansion. Andrew Jackson's subsequent forced removal of the Creek people to Indian Territory (Trail of Tears, 1836-1837) constitutes an act of ethnic cleansing. Thousands of Creek people died during and after removal. The Muscogee (Creek) Nation continues as a federally recognized tribal nation in Oklahoma.

name_variants.name: Creek Confederacy
name_variants.context: English colonial designation, from the Creek ('Ochese Creek') near which early English contact occurred

name_variants.name: Muscogee Confederacy
name_variants.context: Designation using the people's own name, preferred by the modern Muscogee (Creek) Nation

name_variants.name: Muscogee
name_variants.context: Autonym of the core Creek people

territory_changes.region: Southeastern United States (modern Alabama, Georgia, and parts of Florida, Tennessee, South Carolina)
territory_changes.description: The Muscogee Confederacy emerged from the reorganization of southeastern chiefdoms following the collapse of the Mississippian period societies (partly catalyzed by diseases introduced by the de Soto expedition, 1539-1543). The confederacy united Upper Creek towns along the Alabama and Coosa rivers with Lower Creek towns along the Chattahoochee River.

The Treaty of Fort Jackson (August 9, 1814), imposed by Andrew Jackson after the Battle of Horseshoe Bend, forced the cession of approximately 23 million acres (half the Creek homeland), including lands of Creek allies who had fought alongside Jackson. This punitive treaty was applied collectively regardless of which faction individual towns had supported during the Creek War.

The Treaty of Cusseta (1832) ceded remaining Creek lands east of the Mississippi. When the Creek resisted implementation, the U.S. military forcibly removed approximately 15,000 Creek people to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) in 1836-1837. An estimated 3,500 Creek died during the removal and its immediate aftermath.

sources.citation: Ethridge, Robbie. Creek Country: The Creek Indians and Their World. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2003
Saunt, Claudio. A New Order of Things: Property, Power, and the Transformation of the Creek Indians, 1733-1816. Cambridge: Cambridge University Press, 1999
Green, Michael D. The Politics of Indian Removal: Creek Government and Society in Crisis. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1982

ethical_notes:
ETHICS: The Oceti Sakowin (Seven Council Fires), encompassing the Dakota, Lakota, and Nakota peoples, constituted one of the most powerful indigenous political formations in North American history. The term 'Sioux' is an exonym derived from an Ojibwe pejorative and is rejected by many community members, though it remains in official usage (e.g., 'Great Sioux Nation'). The Lakota sacred relationship to the Black Hills (Paha Sapa) is central to their spiritual and cultural identity. The U.S. seizure of the Black Hills in 1877, in violation of the 1868 Fort Laramie Treaty, remains one of the most significant unresolved indigenous land claims. The U.S. Supreme Court ruled in United States v. Sioux Nation (1980) that the seizure was illegal and offered monetary compensation, but the Lakota have refused payment, demanding return of the land. The Wounded Knee Massacre (1890), in which U.S. soldiers killed approximately 250-300 Lakota men, women, and children, constitutes an act of genocide. The ongoing assertion of treaty rights and sovereignty by the Oceti Sakowin nations must be documented.

[v6.7.2] boundary precedente era un mismatch geografico (polygon >10x l'estensione storica attesa). Sostituito con name_seeded_boundary ancorato alla capital. Vedi ETHICS-006.

name_variants.name: Great Sioux Nation
name_variants.context: English diplomatic and treaty designation

name_variants.name: Oceti Sakowin
name_variants.context: Lakota name meaning 'Seven Council Fires', the confederacy's self-designation

name_variants.name: Dakota/Lakota/Nakota
name_variants.context: The three major dialect groups and political divisions of the nation

name_variants.name: Nation Sioux
name_variants.context: French colonial designation

territory_changes.region: Western Great Lakes to the Missouri River
territory_changes.description: The westward expansion of the Lakota and Dakota peoples from the Great Lakes region onto the northern Great Plains, driven partly by Ojibwe pressure and the fur trade. The acquisition of horses and firearms transformed the Lakota into the dominant military power of the northern plains by the mid-18th century. By 1750, the Lakota controlled territory from the Missouri River to the Bighorn Mountains.

The Fort Laramie Treaty of 1868, signed after Red Cloud's War (1866-1868), recognized the Great Sioux Reservation encompassing all of present-day South Dakota west of the Missouri River, including the Black Hills. The treaty was a rare U.S. diplomatic concession to indigenous military power, forced by the defeats at Fetterman Fight (1866) and other engagements.

Following the Great Sioux War of 1876 (including the Lakota/Cheyenne victory at Little Bighorn on June 25, 1876), the U.S. government unilaterally seized the Black Hills and reduced the Great Sioux Reservation by congressional act in February 1877, in direct violation of the 1868 treaty. Sitting Bull fled to Canada; Crazy Horse surrendered and was killed at Fort Robinson in September 1877.

sources.citation: Ostler, Jeffrey. The Lakotas and the Black Hills: The Struggle for Sacred Ground. New York: Viking, 2010
White, Richard. 'The Winning of the West: The Expansion of the Western Sioux in the Eighteenth and Nineteenth Centuries.' Journal of American History 65, no. 2 (1978): 319-343
DeMallie, Raymond J., ed. Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 13: Plains. Washington, D.C.: Smithsonian Institution, 2001
